segments.s645-g49: اگر introduces a hypothetical condition ('if/whether'). صد بمانی literally 'you remain a hundred' uses بمانی (2nd-person present/subjunctive form of ماندن) to mean 'you live/remain (for) a hundred (years)' with the noun 'سال' (years) omitted by ellipsis. وگر is the alternative conditional 'or if' and بیست‌وپنج is the numeral serving the same role as صد (i.e. 'twenty-five (years)').
segments.s645-g50: ببایدت is the classical Persian necessity construction: بباید (it is necessary) plus the enclitic ت marking 'for you' — together 'it is necessary for you' = 'you must'. رفتن is the infinitive complement 'to go/leave' (here used idiomatically for dying or being removed). ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from the place of...', and سپنج is the noun 'sponge' (here 'sponge-bed' or soft bedding); the infinitive follows the finite verb in this older word order.
segments.s645-g51: هر آن چیز is a classical formula 'every/any such thing'. کآید is the relative clause-form of آمدن with the prefixed ک(ه) ('that/which') fused to the verb: 'that comes/comes along'; همی is a classical adverb adding emphasis or habitual sense ('indeed/always'), and در شمار is a prepositional phrase 'into the count/reckoning' (شمار = tally/number). Word order reflects older poetic syntax but means 'every thing that comes into the reckoning'.
segments.s645-g52: سزد‌گر is a compact classical clause meaning 'it is fitting/appropriate if...' (سزد = 'it is fitting', -گر = 'if/should'); نخوانی is 2nd-person present negative/contrary form of خواندن here in the sense 'you call/name' (not 'read'), ورا is the object pronoun 'it' (to it), and پایدار is a predicate adjective 'lasting' — together: 'it is fitting if you do not call it lasting.'
segments.s645-g17: به جای ... نیکی کنی is a prepositional phrase + conditional clause: به جای (in place of) uses به + جای; کسی‌گر is an archaic/compounded conditional sense (گر = 'if') meaning 'if (for) someone'; تو نیکی کنی is a subordinate subjunctive/present-tense clause ('you do/make a good deed'). The verb کنی appears clause-final as usual in Persian poetry.
segments.s645-g18: مزن is a negative imperative ('do not hit/strike') placed before the object phrase; بر سرش is a prepositional phrase ('upon his head') where ش is the enclitic third-person possessive. تا introduces a purposive/negative-result clause ('so that / lest') followed by دلش نشکنی ('you do not break his heart'), with نشکنی a negative subjunctive/optative in clause-final position.
segments.s645-g19: چو is a conditional/temporal particle here ('if/when'); نیکی‌کنش is a noun describing 'one who does good' (a do-gooder / the doing of good) and باشی is the present/subjunctive form 'you are' (clause-initial particle + predicate). بردبار follows as a predicate adjective 'patient'. In poetry the verb and predicates can be arranged compactly with چو marking the condition.
segments.s645-g20: نباشی is the negative counterpart ('you are not'); به چشم خردمند is a prepositional phrase 'in the sight/eye of the wise' with به introducing the viewpoint and خردمند as the possessor; خوار is a predicate/adjective meaning 'despised' or 'held cheap'. The overall sense: even if you do good and are patient, if you are not (truly so) you will be regarded with contempt by the wise.
segments.s645-g21: اگر introduces a condition ('if'); بخت پیروز is a nominal phrase where بخت ('fortune') is qualified by پیروز ('victorious/auspicious') and functions as the subject; یاری دهد is a third-person present/subjunctive verb phrase 'gives help' (دهد at the clause end). The adjective-noun order is typical of Persian poetic register.
segments.s645-g22: مرا is the accusative object 'me' (placed before the verb, as common in Persian); بر جهان is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the world' indicating scope; کامگاری (کام‌گاری) is the goal/result 'success/fulfillment'; دهد is the verb 'gives' appearing clause-final. The sequence shows object + adverbial phrase + object-of-success + verbal predicate typical of classical Persian word order.
segments.s645-g23: یکی is an indefinite article-like word 'a certain' or 'one' introducing the object; دفتری here means 'a book/record' (poetic use of دفتر as 'register'); سازم is the first-person singular present/subjunctive/intentional 'I will make/compose' with the verb at the end. از راستی ('from/of truth') is an ablative/genitive phrase qualifying what the book is made of.
segments.s645-g24: که introduces a relative/purpose clause 'that/which'; بندد is a subjunctive/optative third-person singular 'may bind/shall bind' (clause-final verb); در + کژی و کاستی is the locative/figurative object 'in crookedness and deficiency' meaning the book will 'bind up' or close off faults and shortcomings. The clause expresses purpose/result of composing the book.
segments.s645-g41: همه گوش دارید و فرمان کنید is an address to a group. همه = 'everyone' (subject/vocative); گوش دارید literally 'you have ear' = 'pay attention' (دارید is present 'you have' used with گوش). و links to فرمان کنید, where فرمان is a noun 'command/order' and کنید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative/formal present of کردن meaning 'do/carry out' — here 'obey/carry out the order' or 'give the order' depending on context.
segments.s645-g42: ز پیمان او رامش جان کنید: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' / 'by' (here 'by virtue of' or 'in accordance with'), پیمان او = 'his covenant/pledge' (genitive), رامش جان is a noun phrase 'rest/repose of the soul' (رامش = tranquility, جان = soul) and کنید is the plural imperative 'make/grant' (کنید with کردن). Word order places the object phrase before the verb, as typical in Persian.
segments.s645-g43: اگر چند پیروز با فر و یال uses اگر چند in a classical concessive sense ('even if' / 'though') followed by پیروز 'the victor' modified by a with-phrase با فر و یال ('with fr — royal glory — and yāl — mane/crest'). با marks accompaniment; the phrase qualifies پیروز. The concessive اگر چند is idiomatic in older Persian.
segments.s645-g44: ز هرمز فزونست چندی به سال: ز 'from/by' + هرمز (here the deity Ahura Mazda) is the standard reference; فزونست is an archaic 3rd-singular present 'is more' / 'surpasses'; چندی به سال is an adverbial 'by some years' (چندی = 'several / for some time', به سال = 'in/with respect to years'). The verb фزونست takes the comparison 'ز هرمز' (i.e. 'surpasses Hormuz').
segments.s645-g45: ز هرمز همی‌بینم آهستگی begins with the prepositional/poetic ز 'in/regarding' + هرمز 'Hormuz'; همی‌بینم is the present habitual/continuous 'I see' (first-person singular), and آهستگی is the direct object 'gentleness / slowness' (here 'gentleness' or 'mildness'). The verb governs the following noun phrase, which continues in the next segment.
segments.s645-g46: خردمندی و داد و شایستگی is a simple list of nouns that complete the object of همی‌بینم: خردمندی 'wisdom', داد 'justice' (noun), and شایستگی 'worthiness/competence'. The conjunction و links the items; no new verb is introduced because these nouns are all what the speaker 'sees'.
segments.s645-g47: بگفت این و یک هفته زان پس بزیست: بگفت is a classical past 'he said' (3rd sg.), این 'this' refers to the preceding words, و links the clauses, and یک هفته زان پس بزیست = 'he lived one week after that' where زان پس = 'after that' and بزیست is past 'he lived'. Word order places the duration and temporal phrase before the verb.
segments.s645-g48: برفت و بر او تخت چندی گریست: برفت is an archaic/past-form verb meaning 'he departed / went away' (note: the provided lemma list mislabels it; here it functions as a past verb). و connects clauses; بر او تخت = 'upon his throne' (بر = 'on/upon', او = 'him/his', تخت = 'throne'); چندی 'for a while' modifies گریست, which is past 'he wept'. The clause describes a subsequent action (he went and then for a time wept on his throne).
segments.s645-g9: هر آنکس که introduces a relative clause: 'whoever/anyone who'. دل تیره دارد is a present-tense predicate ‘has a dark heart’ (دارد = present 'has'). ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from' or 'because of', linking رشک (envy) as the source of the dark heart.
segments.s645-g10: مر آن درد را دور باشد is an optative/wish clause addressing the physician: مر = 'to me' (dative), آن درد را = 'that pain/affliction' (را marks it as definite/emphatic), دور باشد = 'may it be far' (بودن in optative/subjunctive sense). پزشک at the end is a vocative address, 'O physician.' The order places the object before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s645-g11: که here functions as a connective ('for/that' or 'because/that'), introducing the consequence. رشک آورد is a present/simple verbal clause 'envy brings/produces' (آورد = 'brings/causes'). The coordinated list آز و گرم و گداز enumerates effects: آز 'greed', گرم 'fervent heat/ardor', گداز 'burning/ardor/anguish', linked by و.
segments.s645-g12: دژآگاه دیوی is a nominal phrase 'the fortress-guardian/detector (دژآگاه) demon (دیو)' — the intervening ی functions as an ezāfe/linker here to join the two nouns. بود is the past copula 'was', and دیرساز is a predicate noun 'delayer' (one who makes things late or causes delay). Word order: subject → copula → predicate noun.
segments.s645-g13: هرآن چیز = 'any/whatever thing'. The following kآنت is an archaic/formal item in this line (textual/older form) that introduces the clause 'that' or qualifies the thing; نیاید پسند literally 'does not come to liking' i.e. 'is not pleasing'. The sequence expresses 'anything that does not please (you)'.
segments.s645-g14: دل دوست و دشمن = 'the heart(s) of friend and enemy'. بر آن = 'toward/against that' (i.e. with respect to that matter). برمبند is a negative-imperative-like admonition 'do not shut/close' (here: do not close your or others' hearts to it). The clause therefore instructs: do not shut the hearts of friends or foes against that unpleasing thing.
segments.s645-g15: مدارا (tolerance/forbearance) is the subject, خرد را (wisdom) is marked by را as the thing compared, and برابر بود = 'was equal'. The construction states an equality: 'Tolerance was equal to wisdom.' را here marks the noun being equated/treated as definite/emphatic.
segments.s645-g16: خرد (wisdom) is the subject; بر سر دانش is a prepositional phrase 'on the head of knowledge' (بر سر = 'on the head of', دانش = 'knowledge'). افسر بود is the nominal predicate 'was a helmet' (افسر = helmet, بود = was). The line metaphorically presents wisdom as the protective helmet of learning.
segments.s645-g25: همی‌داشت is an epic/older imperfective past form (همی + داشت) meaning 'he kept/maintained' or 'he was keeping' — the prefixed همی gives the sense of continued action. یک چند is an adverbial phrase 'for a while / for some time' modifying the verb. گیتی is the direct object 'the world', and به داد is an adverbial phrase 'in justice / with justice' (به used adverbially). Word order places the verb up front, then the duration/adverbial and the object.
segments.s645-g26: زمانه ('time' / 'fortune') is personified as the subject; بدو is the short prepositional clitic 'to him/unto him' (بد + ـو), and شاد is a predicate adjective 'was glad/favorable'. The conjunction و links the next clause: او نیز شاد = 'and he too was glad', with نیز adding 'also/likewise'. The clause order is predicate after subject with a repeated adjective in the coordinated clause.
segments.s645-g27: به هر سو is an adverbial phrase 'to every direction / to every quarter' (به = toward; هر سو = every side). فرستاد is simple past 'he sent / dispatched'. بی‌مر is an archaic/uncertain adjective placed before سپاه; it modifies سپاه (سپاه is 'army, host') and is treated here as an attributive adjective. The whole phrase is 'he sent (or dispatched) a (vast/uncountable) host to every side.'
segments.s645-g28: همی‌داشت again conveys continuous past action 'he kept/was keeping'. گیتی is the object 'the world'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/against', and دشمن نگاه is a genitival phrase 'the enemy's gaze/view' — together 'ز دشمن نگاه' = 'from the enemy's sight/attack' (i.e., protected the world from enemies). Word order places the verb before its object and then the protective prepositional phrase.
segments.s645-g29: ده و هشت here is a compound numeral 'ten and eight' = eighteen; بگذشت is past 'passed' (of time). سال is the time noun 'years'. از introduces the source/measure 'since / over', and برش is an older fused form (بر + ـش) meaning 'on him / over him' (i.e. 'since he was on [the throne]' or 'over him'); this bound/archaic locative is not covered by the simple lemma senses given, so its idiomatic sense is marked separately below.
segments.s645-g30: به پاییز is an adverbial phrase 'toward autumn' and here is metaphorical for 'entering decline / old age'. چون introduces the temporal/similar clause 'as/when', تیره گشت is 'became dark' (تیره 'dark/bleak' + گشت 'became'), and افسرش is 'his helmet' (افسر with possessive suffix ـش). The clause thus says that as he entered his autumn his helmet (a symbol of his state, possibly his crown/armor) darkened.
segments.s645-g31: بزرگان و دانندگان is a coordinated direct object ('the great and the learned'); را is the definite/accusative marker attached to the object. بخواند is the narrative past/archaic verbal form (built with the prefix ب- plus the verb) meaning 'he called/summoned' here, not 'read'. Word order is object(s) + را before the verb, a normal classical Persian SOV pattern.
segments.s645-g32: بر تخت زرین is a prepositional phrase 'upon the golden throne' (noun + postpositive adjective زرین). به زانو indicates the place/result 'onto the knee/into his lap.' نشاند is the archaic past (3rd sg.) of نشاندن 'to set/make sit' and completes the transitive action whose object was marked by را in the previous segment.
segments.s645-g33: چنین گفت is a formulaic, poetic clause meaning “thus said.” In classical Persian narrative, گفت (past of گفتن) follows چنین to introduce direct speech; کین is an archaic demonstrative (“this/that”) modifying چرخ, and ناپایدار is a postpositive adjective describing that noun (“the inconstant/fickle wheel” — i.e. fate). Word order is formulaic: adverbial formula + verb + demonstrative + noun + adjective.
segments.s645-g34: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negative ‘neither ... nor ...’. پرورده is a past-participle adjective meaning “nurtured/produced,” while پرودگار is a noun meaning “nourisher/sustainer.” داند here is a literary present/auxiliary used in the predicate; the phrase asserts that the wheel is neither (a) something that is nurtured nor (b) a nurturer — a compact poetical copular/nominal clause.
segments.s645-g35: به + noun marks the object or target of the verb (here ‘toward/at the crowns’). ننگرد is a literary negative present (ن‑ + نگرَد) meaning “does not/look or regard not” and comes at the end of the clause in customary verb-final order. تاج گرانمایگان is a genitival phrase ‘the crown(s) of the great/illustrious’, with گرانمایگان as an epithet/noun meaning ‘the eminent’.
segments.s645-g36: This is a relative clause introduced by که ‘who/that’: شکاری (an agent noun from شکار) means ‘a hunter’ (or one who takes possession), که یابد همی is ‘who ever finds/obtains it’ — یابد is literary present/subjunctive meaning ‘finds/attains’, with همی adding iterative/continuous or emphatic aspect. بشکرد is an archaic verb form meaning ‘will break/shatter’ and appears at clause end as the main verb (verb-final order).
segments.s645-g37: کنون is a temporal adverb ‘now’. روز من ‘my day’ (روز as ‘time/era’) is the subject of the idiomatic predicate بر سر آید, literally ‘comes upon the head’ — an idiom meaning one’s day has come to a climax or end. آید is the literary present/near-future verb ‘comes’, and همی is an archaic/emphatic particle marking continual or imminent aspect.
segments.s645-g38: به نیرو is an instrumental phrase ‘by/with force’ (به used here as ‘by’), شکست is a nominal/abstract ‘breaking/defeat’, and اندر آید همی is a verb phrase ‘comes into / will come upon’ with اندر as a locative/introductory particle. Overall the clause says that by force a breaking/defeat will come — the verb آید ends the clause and همی again gives iterative/emphatic colouring.
segments.s645-g39: سپردم is the first-person singular past ‘I entrusted/handed over.’ به marks the beneficiary/recipient (‘to Hormuz’). کلاه و نگین are the direct objects listed (kalaah literally ‘cap/hat’, نگین ‘gem/signet’). Word order: verb-subject-object in the compact epic narrative results in the verb starting the clause.
segments.s645-g40: همه modifies the coordinated noun phrase لشکر و گنج ایران‌زمین ‘the army and the treasure of the Iranian realm’, i.e. ‘all the army and treasure of Iran.’ The conjunction و links the two nouns; ایران‌زمین is a compound proper noun ‘land of Iran’. This phrase completes the list of things entrusted.
segments.s645-g1: چو (when) + شد (past of شدن) introduces a temporal clause: 'when he became'. پادشا (pādshā, an archaic/poetic form of پادشاه) is the predicate-noun and یزدگرد is the ruler's name in apposition; بر جهان is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the world' describing the scope of his kingship. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (verb 'شد' follows the temporal particle).
segments.s645-g2: سپاه پراگنده را is the definite object (را marks it); پراگنده postmodifies سپاه. The verb complex گرد کردن (to gather, literally 'make round') appears split as کرد گرد — a common poetic inversion where کرد (past of کردن) combines with گرد to mean 'assembled/gathered'.
segments.s645-g3: نشستند is third-person plural past of نشستن ('they sat'); با introduces accompaniment ('with'), linking the subject to the following groups. موبدان و ردان are coordinated companions; و simply links them. (Note: the supplied senses list for نشستند/ردان does not match the usual meanings, so one of those entries is flagged below.)
segments.s645-g4: A further coordination of titles/groups: بزرگان 'the great ones' + و + سالاروش (an epithet/leader; here 'commander/leader') followed by بخردان, an adjective/noun meaning 'the wise'. The sequence lists the notable people attending; word order is a simple coordination typical of prose rendered in verse.
segments.s645-g5: جهانجوی is a compound noun/adjective 'world-seeking' (an ambitious person) serving as the subject. بر تخت زرین is a locative phrase ('upon the golden throne') with the adjective زرین after the noun (poetic adjective placement). نشست is the past verb 'sat' (3rd sing.).
segments.s645-g6: در رنج is an adverbial phrase 'in/with hardship'; the coordinating و links to the following clause. دست بدی را is the definite object (را marks it) meaning 'the hand/power of evil', and ببست is the past verb 'he bound/closed'. Together this says he curtailed the power of evil while in hardship. Note the idiomatic/metaphorical use of دست = 'power/agency'.
segments.s645-g7: نخستین 'first' + چنین 'thus' frames reported speech; گفت here introduces what was said. The following کن is an imperative ('do!') that begins the quoted instruction. کز is the subordinating particle (که از) introducing the content/purpose ('from which/from which one is freed of sin') and governs گناه. In verse the reporting verb (گفت) is often immediately followed by the imperative to mark direct speech.
segments.s645-g8: برآسود شد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'was/were freed/at rest' (برآسود + شد = 'became at rest/freed'); ایمن از is an adjectival phrase 'safe from', and کینه‌خواه is the agent 'the avenger' or 'the one who seeks vengeance'. Grammatically this completes the imperative's content: do that by which one is freed from sin and thus safe from the avenger.

sentences.s645-s25: Whether you live a hundred years or only twenty-five, you must leave the sponge-bed.
sentences.s645-s26: Anything that comes into the reckoning is worthy of being counted, even if you would not call it lasting.
sentences.s645-s9: If you do a kindness in place of someone, do not strike his head so that you do not break his heart.
sentences.s645-s10: When you are a doer of good and patient, yet are not, in the eyes of the wise, held in contempt.
sentences.s645-s11: If fortunate fate grants victorious help, and grants me success over the world.
sentences.s645-s12: I will compose a book of truth that will bind up crookedness and deficiency.
sentences.s645-s21: Everyone, listen and obey; by his covenant, grant repose to his soul.
sentences.s645-s22: Even if the victor, with splendour and mane, surpasses Hormuz by several years.
sentences.s645-s23: In Hormuz I perceive gentleness, wisdom, justice, and worthiness.
sentences.s645-s24: He said this and lived a week thereafter; he departed and, upon his throne, wept for a while.
sentences.s645-s5: Whoever has a dark heart from envy — may that affliction be far from me, O physician.
sentences.s645-s6: For envy brings greed, ardor and burning anguish; the fortress-warden demon was a delayer.
sentences.s645-s7: Do not close the heart of friend or foe over anything that does not please you.
sentences.s645-s8: Tolerance was equal to wisdom; wisdom was a helmet upon the head of knowledge.
sentences.s645-s13: For a while he maintained the world in justice; the age (fortune) was favorable to him, and he too was happy.
sentences.s645-s14: He dispatched an army to every quarter; he kept the world from the enemy's gaze.
sentences.s645-s15: Eighteen years passed over him; when he reached autumn (his decline) his helmet grew dark.
sentences.s645-s16: He summoned the great and the learned and seated them on the golden throne at his knee.
sentences.s645-s17: Thus he said: this unstable wheel is neither a product (nurtured) nor a nurturer.
sentences.s645-s18: It does not regard the crowns of the exalted; a hunter who gains it will shatter it.
sentences.s645-s19: Now my day is coming to its head; by force it will be broken.
sentences.s645-s20: I have entrusted to Hormuz the cap and the gem — all the army and the treasure of the land of Iran.
sentences.s645-s1: When Yazdegerd became king over the world, he gathered together the scattered army.
sentences.s645-s2: They sat with the mobeds and the rēdān, the great men and their commanders and the wise.
sentences.s645-s3: The ambitious (world-seeking) man sat on the golden throne; in hardship he bound the hand of evil.
sentences.s645-s4: First he said thus: 'Do (this) — from sin be freed; you will be safe from the avenger.'
